skill.name: routeros-app-yaml
skill.description: RouterOS /app YAML format for container applications (7.21+ builtin app, 7.22+ custom YAML creation). Use when: writing or validating RouterOS /app YAML files, working with MikroTik container apps, building docker-compose-like definitions for RouterOS, creating /app store schemas, debugging /app validation errors, or when the user mentions /app, tikapp, or RouterOS container YAML.
skill.summary: Documentation and examples for RouterOS /app YAML container application format (MikroTik RouterOS 7.21+).
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: This skill contains documentation-only content (markdown files with YAML examples) for RouterOS /app container application format. All static analyzer findings are false positives: backtick patterns are YAML code blocks in markdown, URLs and IPs are documentation examples, and there is no executable code. No security risks detected.

content.value_statement: RouterOS container administrators need help writing valid /app YAML definitions for MikroTik routers. This skill provides documentation and examples for the RouterOS /app YAML format, enabling correct container application configurations.
actual_capabilities: Write valid RouterOS /app YAML definitions for container applications
Explain port mapping formats (OCI-style and RouterOS 7.23+ colon-style)
Document placeholder usage for dynamic IP and port expansion
Create /app store schema files for container collections
Validate YAML against RouterOS schema requirements
Debug /app validation errors and configuration issues
limitations: Documentation skill only - does not execute RouterOS commands or deploy containers
Requires RouterOS 7.21+ with container package installed for actual deployment
Does not validate custom Docker images or external dependencies
Schema validation references external JSON schemas not included in this skill

use_cases.title: Network Administrator
use_cases.description: Deploy self-hosted services on MikroTik RouterOS devices using native /app container format
use_cases.target_user: RouterOS administrators managing home or business networks

use_cases.title: Home Lab Enthusiast
use_cases.description: Create docker-compose-like YAML definitions optimized for RouterOS container limitations
use_cases.target_user: Self-hosters running services on MikroTik hardware

use_cases.title: Developer
use_cases.description: Package applications as RouterOS /app store entries with proper YAML schemas
use_cases.target_user: Developers publishing container apps for RouterOS ecosystem

prompt_templates.title: Basic /app Definition
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a minimal RouterOS /app YAML for an nginx web server on port 8080.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner - Quick start with simple container deployment

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Service Application
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a complete /app YAML with a web service and PostgreSQL database, including volumes, networks, and health checks.

prompt_templates.title: Port Mapping Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the difference between RouterOS port formats and show examples of both OCI-style and 7.23+ colon-style with placeholders.

prompt_templates.title: App Store Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a .tikappstore.yaml file containing three related container apps with proper category and metadata fields.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced - Publishing to RouterOS app stores

output_examples.output: A YAML configuration with name, services section containing nginx image, and port mapping 8080:80/tcp

output_examples.input: How do I use placeholders in port mappings?
output_examples.output: Documentation showing [accessIP], [accessPort], [containerIP], and [routerIP] placeholders with example YAML snippets for both port format styles

best_practices: Use the .tikapp.yaml extension for custom app files to follow RouterOS conventions
Specify restart policies (always, unless-stopped) for production container reliability
Include health checks for services that need availability monitoring
anti_patterns: Adding version field (deprecated and ignored by RouterOS)
Using docker-compose deploy or resources sections (not supported)
Mixing old OCI-style and new colon-style port formats in the same entry

faq.question: What RouterOS version supports /app YAML?
faq.answer: RouterOS 7.21 introduced /app path for built-in apps. Full YAML creation via /app/add requires RouterOS 7.22 or later.

faq.question: Do I need the container package installed?
faq.answer: Yes. The /app endpoint requires the container extra package. Without it, /app API calls return 404 errors.

faq.question: Is this format compatible with docker-compose?
faq.answer: Partially. RouterOS /app uses similar YAML structure but has different port format, limited volume options, and no deploy section. Do not assume full compatibility.

faq.question: What port mapping format should I use?
faq.answer: Both formats work. New 7.23+ colon-style (8080:80:web:tcp) is preferred for new apps. Old OCI-style (8080:80/tcp) maintains backward compatibility.

faq.question: How do placeholders expand at deploy time?
faq.answer: Placeholders like [accessIP] and [accessPort] are replaced with actual values when RouterOS parses the YAML. They appear in port mappings, environment values, and config content.

faq.question: Where are custom app YAML files stored?
faq.answer: Custom apps are loaded via URL (yaml-url parameter) or inline YAML. The container_name determines file paths under /container directory on RouterOS.
